title: Elasticities (PED, YED, XED, PES)
summary:
## The big idea Demand and supply tell you *which way* price and quantity move. **Elasticity** tells you *by how much* — it turns the diagrams into numbers you can actually use. Every elasticity is built the same way: > **elasticity = % change in the variable affected ÷ % change in the variable that caused it** You must know four: **PED**, **YED** and **XED** on the demand side, and **PES** on the supply side. Between them they answer questions firms and governments ask every day: *if we raise the price, will revenue rise or fall? If incomes grow, which products will boom? If our rival cuts their price, how badly does it hurt us?* ## Price elasticity of demand (PED) **PED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price.** Because demand curves slope down, PED is normally **negative** — a price *rise* causes a quantity *fall*. Keep the sign in your working, then talk about the *size* (the magnitude). A quick worked example: the price of a chocolate bar rises by 10% and the quantity demanded falls by 5%. PED = −5% ÷ 10% = **−0.5**. The size (0.5) is less than 1, so demand is **inelastic**: buyers barely react. | Value of PED | Term | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 0 | Perfectly inelastic | Quantity doesn't change at all | | between 0 and −1 | Inelastic | Quantity changes proportionately *less* than price | | exactly −1 | Unit elastic | Quantity changes in exact proportion | | beyond −1 (e.g. −3) | Elastic | Quantity changes proportionately *more* than price | | infinite | Perfectly elastic | Any price rise wipes out demand completely |
**What each part means:** both demand curves pass through the same starting point (the orange dot) at price **P1** and quantity **Q1**. The price then rises to **P2**. On the **steep red curve (inelastic)** quantity barely falls — only to **Qin**. On the **shallow yellow curve (elastic)** the same price rise cuts quantity all the way back to **Qel**. Steeper = less responsive; flatter = more responsive (at any given point).
### What makes demand elastic or inelastic? (SPLAT) - **S**ubstitutes — more (and closer) substitutes → more elastic. If one brand of bottled water gets dearer, you just buy another. - **P**roportion of income — goods that take a big share of income (cars, holidays) → more elastic; cheap items (salt) → inelastic. - **L**uxury or necessity — luxuries are elastic; necessities (basic food, fuel, medicine) are inelastic. - **A**ddictive or habit-forming — cigarettes and coffee are inelastic. - **T**ime — the longer buyers have to adjust and find alternatives, the **more elastic** demand becomes. ### PED and total revenue — the money question **Total revenue (TR) = price × quantity.** A price change pulls TR in two directions at once (higher price per unit, but fewer units), and PED tells you which force wins: - Demand **inelastic** → a price **rise** raises TR (quantity barely falls). - Demand **elastic** → a price **cut** raises TR (quantity jumps a lot). - **Unit elastic** → TR doesn't change. Worked example: a firm sells 100 units at \$10, so TR = \$1,000. PED is −0.5. It raises price 10% to \$11; quantity falls 5% to 95 units. New TR = \$11 × 95 = **\$1,045** — revenue *rose*. This single link explains why rail firms raise peak fares, and why governments tax cigarettes: with inelastic demand, price rises bring in more money. ## Income elasticity of demand (YED) **YED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in income.** The **sign** now carries the meaning: - **Normal goods** have **positive** YED — demand rises with income. Within these, **necessities** have YED between 0 and 1 (food) and **luxuries** have YED above 1 (foreign holidays, restaurant meals). - **Inferior goods** have **negative** YED — as incomes rise, buyers switch away (own-brand groceries, bus journeys in some countries). Firms use YED to plan for booms and recessions: in a downturn, luxury sellers suffer most while sellers of inferior goods may actually gain. It also explains **structural change** — as a country like China or India grows richer, demand shifts from basic goods towards services. ## Cross elasticity of demand (XED) **XED = % change in quantity demanded of good A ÷ % change in price of good B.** Again the **sign** is the message: - **Substitutes → positive** XED: if Pepsi's price rises, demand for Coca-Cola rises. - **Complements → negative** XED: if printers get cheaper, demand for ink cartridges rises. - **Unrelated goods → XED ≈ 0.** The *size* shows how close the relationship is — an XED of +2 means very close substitutes; +0.1 means barely related. ## Price elasticity of supply (PES) **PES = % change in quantity supplied ÷ % change in price** — normally **positive**, because supply curves slope up. Supply is elastic when producers can raise output quickly and cheaply. The determinants: - **Spare capacity** — idle machines and workers → elastic. - **Stocks** — goods that can be stored and released → elastic. - **Ease of switching** production between products → more elastic. - **Factor mobility** — how quickly labour/capital can move into the industry. - **Time** — the key one: supply is **inelastic in the short run** and **more elastic in the long run**. Agriculture is the classic case: once the season's crop is planted, a price spike cannot conjure more coffee this year — but plantations can expand over several years. ## Real-world application Elasticity is everywhere in policy. Governments deliberately tax **inelastic** goods (tobacco, fuel) because quantity falls little and revenue is reliable. OPEC's power comes from oil's short-run inelastic demand *and* supply — small output cuts cause big price spikes, as in 2022. And supermarkets price own-brand basics keenly because their demand is highly elastic.
## ⚡ Quick revision - All elasticities = **% change in effect ÷ % change in cause**. - **PED** (negative): size < 1 inelastic, > 1 elastic. Determinants = **SPLAT**. - **PED ↔ TR**: inelastic → raise price to raise TR; elastic → cut price to raise TR; unit elastic → TR unchanged. - **YED**: positive = normal (0–1 necessity, >1 luxury); negative = inferior. - **XED**: positive = substitutes; negative = complements; ≈0 = unrelated. - **PES** (positive): depends on spare capacity, stocks, switching, factor mobility, **time** — more elastic in the long run. - In calculations: show the **formula, working and sign**.

keyTerms.definition: The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in the good's own price.
PED between 0 and −1: quantity changes proportionately less than price.
PED beyond −1: quantity changes proportionately more than price.
The responsiveness of demand to a change in consumer income.
A good with positive YED — demand rises as income rises.
A good with negative YED — demand falls as income rises.
The responsiveness of demand for one good to a price change of another.
The responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price.
Price × quantity sold; its response to a price change depends on PED.

diagrams: **PED extremes:** draw a vertical demand curve (perfectly inelastic) and a horizontal one (perfectly elastic); show a steep (inelastic) vs shallow (elastic) curve.
**PED & total revenue:** on a linear demand curve, shade the TR rectangle and show how it changes when price falls in the elastic vs inelastic range.
**PES:** draw a steep (inelastic) and a shallow (elastic) supply curve; a supply line through the origin is unit elastic.
tips: Show the **formula and working** in calculations and keep the **sign** (negative PED/XED tells you the relationship — don't just drop it).
Link **PED to total revenue**: inelastic → raise price; elastic → cut price. A classic application question.
Use **SPLAT** to generate determinants of PED quickly and fully.
Remember supply (and demand) are **more elastic in the long run** — always consider the time period.
Apply elasticity to policy: governments tax **inelastic** demerit goods (tobacco) to raise revenue with little fall in quantity — a strong evaluation link.
revisionNotes:
# Elasticities — quick revision

**The master formula**
- Every elasticity = **% change in the affected variable ÷ % change in the causing variable**.

**PED = %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP** (negative)
- Size < 1 → **inelastic**; size > 1 → **elastic**; = 1 unit elastic; 0 and ∞ are the perfect extremes.
- Determinants — **SPLAT**: Substitutes, Proportion of income, Luxury/necessity, Addictiveness, Time.
- **Total revenue rule**: inelastic → raise price ↑TR; elastic → cut price ↑TR; unit elastic → TR unchanged.

**YED = %ΔQd ÷ %ΔY**
- Positive = **normal** good (0–1 necessity; >1 luxury). Negative = **inferior** good.

**XED = %ΔQd of A ÷ %ΔP of B**
- Positive = **substitutes**; negative = **complements**; near 0 = unrelated. Bigger size = closer relationship.

**PES = %ΔQs ÷ %ΔP** (positive)
- Elastic if: spare capacity, stocks available, easy switching, mobile factors, **long time period**.
- Agriculture: inelastic in the short run (growing seasons).

**Exam habits**
- Always show formula + working; keep the sign; state elastic/inelastic and *what that means*.
- Link PED to tax policy (tax inelastic goods) and firm pricing.
questions:
# Practice questions — Elasticities **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** The price of a good rises by 10% and the quantity demanded falls by 25%. The price elasticity of demand is: - A — −0.4 and inelastic - B — −2.5 and elastic - C — +2.5 and elastic - D — −2.5 and inelastic
▸ Show answer

B. PED = −25% ÷ 10% = −2.5. The size (2.5) is greater than 1, so demand is elastic. C has the wrong sign; A inverts the formula.

**2.** The income elasticity of demand for good X is −0.4. Good X is: - A — a luxury good - B — a necessity - C — an inferior good - D — a complement
▸ Show answer

C. Negative YED means demand falls as income rises — the definition of an inferior good. Luxuries are YED > 1; necessities 0–1; complements relate to XED, not YED.

**3.** The cross elasticity of demand between goods A and B is +1.8. The two goods are: - A — close substitutes - B — weak substitutes - C — close complements - D — unrelated
▸ Show answer

A. A positive XED means substitutes; a size of 1.8 is large, so they are close substitutes. Complements would give a negative value.

**4.** A firm faces price-inelastic demand for its product. To increase total revenue it should: - A — lower its price - B — raise its price - C — leave price unchanged - D — increase output
▸ Show answer

B. With inelastic demand, quantity falls proportionately less than price rises, so total revenue (P × Q) increases when price rises.

## Paper 2 style — data response > A city bus company raises the standard fare from \$1.00 to \$1.20. Over the following year, passenger journeys fall from 50 million to 45 million. A transport economist comments: 'Many passengers have no realistic alternative for getting to work, at least in the short run.' **(a)** Define 'price elasticity of demand'. **[2]** **(b)** Calculate the price elasticity of demand for bus journeys and comment on your result. **[4]** **(c)** Explain **two** reasons why demand for bus travel might be price inelastic. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether raising fares is likely to remain a successful way for the company to increase revenue in the long run. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in the good's own price; formula %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP. [1+1]

(b) %ΔP = +20%; %ΔQd = −10%; PED = −10 ÷ 20 = −0.5. Size < 1 so demand is inelastic — and indeed revenue rises from \$50m to \$54m. [formula/working 2, result 1, comment 1]

(c) Any two SPLAT-style reasons applied to buses: few substitutes for commuters; fares a small share of income; travel to work is a necessity; short time to adjust. Develop each. [3+3]

(d) For: inelastic demand → higher fares raise revenue now. Against: in the long run demand becomes more elastic — passengers buy bikes/scooters, car-share, move, or switch to remote work; repeated rises may attract competitor services or regulation. Judgement: works short run, weakens over time. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain the factors that determine the price elasticity of demand for a product. **[8]** **(b)** Assess how useful estimates of price elasticity of demand are to a firm deciding its pricing strategy. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) SPLAT with clear explanation and an example for each factor used; link each to 'more/less elastic'.

(b) Useful: predicts revenue effects of price changes (TR rule); helps segment markets (peak/off-peak). Limits: estimates come from past data and may be outdated; ceteris paribus rarely holds (rivals react, incomes change); elasticity varies along the curve and over time. Judgement: a guide, not a guarantee.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, with examples, the difference between income elasticity of demand and cross elasticity of demand. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether knowledge of elasticities is more useful to a government than to a firm. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Define both with formulas; signs carry meaning (YED: normal/inferior; XED: substitutes/complements); one clear example of each.

(b) Government uses: taxing inelastic goods for revenue, predicting effects of subsidies, devaluation (trade elasticities). Firm uses: pricing, forecasting demand as incomes change, reacting to rivals. Both face the same data problems. A justified judgement either way scores — e.g. both essential, but reliability limits each.
